This is a week early, but it makes sense to do with a minor point bump, which #1525 requires too, so doing them together makes sense.

I wouldn't check if `value` is a string comparing `type(value)` to `str` directly. Instead just use
```
if not isinstance(value, str):
    value = ""
```
This is because `isinstance()` function [consider inherited classes accroding to Liskov Susbs. principle](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1549801/what-are-the-differences-between-type-and-isinstance) unlike direct type comparision:
```
>>> class Bar: 
...     pass
>>> class Foo(Bar):  # child class
...     pass
>>> f = Foo()
>>> isinstance(f, Bar)
True
>>> type(f) == Bar
False  # that is the point
```

Also, could we just let it be something like:
```
    def __str__(self):
        # just set empty string as a default for __getattr__()
        value = str(getattr(self, "name", "")
                    or getattr(self, "label", "")
                    or getattr(self, "display", ""))
        return value
```
?
I've tested the code above, it works correct.

I'd appreciate it if you updated your PR considering the points i described. 
This PR fixes a real import bug and should be merged.

Thanks for raising this! I've made a few comments.

Dask has a config system that we can use. 

- Default values [go in here](https://github.com/dask/dask-kubernetes/blob/main/dask_kubernetes/kubernetes.yaml). 
- We should also add a [kwarg to the class](https://github.com/dask/dask-kubernetes/blob/dd0a112d6f37a84a7b4f0e1f761b53e628e3da01/dask_kubernetes/core.py#L400). 
- Then set an attribute [like this](https://github.com/dask/dask-kubernetes/blob/main/dask_kubernetes/core.py#L442-L444). 
- The attribute can then be passed on to our the `get_external_address_for_scheduler_service` call.
I wonder if we should drop support for 3.5, since it’s end of life, though. But even if we did, it’s probably better to handle that separately.

It's a good refactoring.

Though, `get_conatiner_process_table()` could be reimplemented using `/proc/{pid}/status`'s `NSPid` field (ref: https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man5/proc.5.html) following `get_host_process_table()`, as long as we're on the Linux kernel 4.1 or later. (A notable exception is CentOS 7... and we may keep the current implementation as a fallback for CentOS 7 users and macOS development setups.)

It would be better to avoid spawning subprocesses for this kind of simple queries whenever possible. Even the mapping query itself could be rewritten to just read a single line of `/proc/{pid}/status` without contacting the Docker daemon API at all.

I think we could rewrite `get_container_process_table()` as a follow-up PR.

Thanks @JoshuaC3 ! I've read the attached issue and the other stuff it links to so I think I understand what this is trying to accomplish, but I'm concerned.

`init_score` can be an array with shape `(n_observations, )` or even `(n_observations, n_classes)` for multiclass classification ([docs](https://lightgbm.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pythonapi/lightgbm.Dataset.html#lightgbm.Dataset)), so this could be a fairly large amount of data. This might substantially increase the memory footprint of the Booster object if you used `init_score` during training, which would have at least two negative side effects:

* increases the memory requirements for deploying a LightGBM model
* increases the amount of data that needs to be sent over the wire from Dask workers back to the client in distributed training with `lightgbm.dask` (https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/d5c2c55682cdf52b9371dbead9bd551e2acbffdc/python-package/lightgbm/dask.py)

Let's wait to merge this until we've fully thought it through.

The issue with this PR is that we always determine interpreter constraints for Flake8 based on your own code's constraints. This is because Flake8 behavior matters depending on the interpreter you use it with. If you run w/ 2.7, you get different results than Py3. Pants has support for things like running some of your codebase w/ Py27 w/ Flake8 and some w/ Py3 - which is a really powerful feature. I think we want to keep this runtime behavior.

When generating the lockfile for your code, we thus use your own code's ICs. This PR would change it to instead use the subsystem option, but that option would only be used when generating the lockfile and not during consumption.

Instead, I suspect what's happening is Toolchain wants to use Flake8 6, and part of its codebase (a Pants plugin) is set to work w/ Python 3.7, whereas the rest of the codebase uses Py38+. The `generate-lockfiles` error is legit! It won't be able to run Flake8 on that part of the codebase. If Toolchain wanted to use Flake8 6, then it would need to ensure every single target was using Python 3.8+.

When `strict := False`, underscores are be valid in all labels, in any position, even first position, according to the chromium/djb code path.

I can see how what you've implemented suits a case different from the chromium/djb code: For domains used for web browsing, the CAs are [not supposed to](https://github.com/ypcrts/fqdn/issues/14#issuecomment-688604160) sign certs that have an underscore in the apex domain name or public suffix (i.e. TLD).  TLS is so important in 2020 that I see the CAs agreement as almost defining what a valid domain name is now, but originally your case for this option was based off what browsers accept, not what CAs accept. As for what is valid in the real world, I think Chromium source is a good authority. Is there another conflicting authority?

If you have a use case for an option that determines whether underscores are allowed as any of the first char of a label or in the apex domain or TLD, then I'd like to see an authority for that.

I would prefer that we simply match the chromium/djb path with this option.  If you want to make that change, I'll accept it. And like you said in #14, the name `allow_underscores` suits this better, but I can port it over after merge.

Thanks for your work on this again

I'm currently thinking about how to can keep our API correct and consistent in the long run. How do we justify adding the the `mirror` parameter only to some of our functions that use a `footprint` whose symmetry would impact their output. Rather than let a bug influence our API I would prefer to skip the perhaps a bit trivial `mirror` parameter and enable the user to do so beforehand with `mirror_footprint`.

Maybe we could add `mirror` temporarily to `binary_dilation` as part of a deprecation path? E.g.

- Release x: In `binary_dilation`, add `mirror=True`. Using `True` will raise a `FutureWarning` that mirroring will be removed in the future. Advise the user to use `mirror=False` and apply `mirror_footprint` if they need it.
- Release x+1: Remove the mirroring behavior. Using the `mirror` parameter at all raises a `FutureWarning` that the parameter is deprecated. 
- Release x+2: Remove the `mirror` parameter.

For users that use `footprint=None` anyway, we could reduce the noise by not raising deprecation warnings at all in that case. What do you think?

Hmm—you only need an event file at the run directory, right?

```
$ find /tmp/proj/mnist/lr*
/tmp/proj/mnist1/lr_1E-03,conv=1,fc=2
/tmp/proj/mnist1/lr_1E-03,conv=1,fc=2/events.out.tfevents.1563406327.HOSTNAME
/tmp/proj/mnist1/lr_1E-03,conv=1,fc=2/plugins
/tmp/proj/mnist1/lr_1E-03,conv=1,fc=2/plugins/org_tensorflow_tensorboard_projector
/tmp/proj/mnist1/lr_1E-03,conv=1,fc=2/plugins/org_tensorflow_tensorboard_projector/projector_config.pbtxt
$ bazel run //tensorboard -- --logdir /tmp/proj  # works
```

You specifically should not need it for the plugin assets directory
itself: i.e., `projector_config.pbtxt` does not need to have any event
files as siblings.

I came across another general issue. We currently filter out all unplayable tracks and this means we'll be removing them whenever we make any playlist change, which is wrong. Tracks might be currently unplayable if unavailable in the geo region where the user is logged in (but may be playable from elsewhere and/or in the future). I think local files might also be treated as unplayable but I'm not sure if Spotify still supports this feature I never used it. I think to workaround this we'll need to change the current playlist behaviour (which has been the same since our first implementation) to now include unplayable tracks in playlists. libspotify won't be able to load these tracks when it comes to try and play them and they will be skipped with an `info` log message (which we will want to adjust). These unplayable tracks will clutter the queue/playlist listing and might be confusing to users when they try to play them. But I don't see another way to avoid losing them when we use `save()`.

I've not looked closely at the myers implementation here, I assume it's the normal one that's available everywhere. It seems odd that in 2020 there is no standard, fast (C), robust implementation available for us to just pull in and use. It's not ideal we have to reproduce (and test) our own version of this common algorithm. But we do have Python's standard `difflib.SequenceMatcher`, so what does using myers provide over just using that? I did find multiple (why???) packages for Google's [diff-match-patch(https://github.com/google/diff-match-patch) library. At a higher level, some of what has been added to playlist.py belongs in web.py. You'll notice there isn't currently any Spotify Web API specific stuff in playlist.py. I think we want to keep that separation.

Thanks!  Nice work finding out where to make these changes.  This is all good practice at making pull requests :)

I know this is a pretty straightforward change, since it's only to documentation, but attention to detail is still important!  These are small details, but they'll need to get fixed up before merging.

One important detail: when making a pull request, you [always want to start by making a new branch](https://guides.github.com/introduction/flow/).  This PR is made from the `main` branch of your repo, which will make it hard to do other work in your repo, since `main` should always be the latest version of the main repository.

One last point about the pull request -- the title should describe very briefly what it does.  In this case, that might be "Document that index paths cannot contain slashes".  That way when we're looking through long lists of PRs, we can know quickly what this one is.  "Documentation add" doesn't mean much, and "Issue #3721" is only helpful if we remember what that issue number means.  It's usually good to add a bit longer explanation in the PR description: why this change is made, and where the documentation will appear.  And if you end the description with "Fixes #3721" then GitHub will automatically link the issue and the PR, and will even close the issue when the PR is merged.

Unfortunately, it's not possible to change the branch from which a pull request is made, so we'll need to close this one up.  Next steps:
 * create a new branch in your repo (I usually name mine after the issue, e.g., `issue3721`)
 * make the changes and commit them (think of a good commit message!)
 * make a new PR

If you're still working on getting your environment set up, then we can run `yarn generate` for you and push the changes to your branch.  I can't push changes to your `main` branch, so I can't do it for you on this PR.

This breaks `certbot update_account -m email@example.com`, because the signature of `update_registration` is:

```python
    def update_registration(self, regr: messages.RegistrationResource,
                            update: Optional[messages.Registration] = None
                            ) -> messages.RegistrationResource:
```

The first argument being the existing `regr`, and  the second argument being the changes you actually want to make.

Unfortunately, `main.update_account` calls `update_registration(regr.update(...))`, instead of `update_registration(regr, regr.body.update(contact=...))`.

The former method is broken by the changes in this PR, because `_get_v2_account` now overwrites the body of the `regr` (the first argument) with what is sent by the ACME server. This wipes out the change to `contact` queued up by `main.update_account`. 

This is correct behavior in my opinion, but arguably a breaking change to the `acme` module's API.

We could fix the way `main.update_account` calls `update_registration`, but we'd still be potentially breaking other API users who were relying on the old behavior of `update_registration`.

Thanks for your interest in LightGBM. I'm personally unsure about the value of adding this to LightGBM. I understand the statement from #5177...

> Random assignment for a variable V in a tree is a way to drop the effect of that variable in the model

But what is the value of this being **in LightGBM**, instead of having user code or some other library just produce input with randomly-varied feature values and then using LightGBM's existing `predict()` functions?

We have a very small team of maintainers here with a very small amount of time and effort available relative to the size of the project, and I'm personally not convinced that this new functionality is something we should take into LightGBM.

I'm only one vote though, will defer to @guolinke's and @shiyu1994 's opinion.

-----

If we do move forward with this, I expect to see this PR also add unit tests confirming that the functionality works as expected. Since it's new functionality, I'd expect that none of LightGBM's existing unit tests should need to be modified at all.

I'd like to see tests covering all of the following cases:

* random assignment of one feature
* random assignment of all features
* `random_assign_features` contains feature indices that are too large (e.g. model has 10 features and you pass `random_assign_features=27`)
* `random_assign_features` contains duplicates of the same feature
* `random_assign_features` works as expected with all of LightGBM's prediction types:
    - raw score
    - feature contributions (`pred_contrib`)
    - leaf index (`pred_leaf_index`)

The name and location of this handler rub me the wrong way, since we'd shoehorn this into the `conda` namespace, making it "private" using the leading underscore *and* overwrite the base class with a dynamically created subclass.

I think that's far too exciting for this functionality, it should not get in the way and not risk the runtime issues of the decorated code. It's as if we're telling people importing conda (e.g. 3rd party developers) that they shouldn't use it, but it's still there, just for the conda code itself and nothing else.

I would suggest doing the following:

- move this into a new module `conda/deprecations.py` (analog to `conda/exceptions.py`)
- remove the leading underscore to enable other conda libraries to depend on it to deprecate things
- rewrite it using `wrapt` since [it's the most correct solution](https://hynek.me/articles/decorators/) for writing decorators and we need to be correct (`functools.wraps` isn't enough)

We _could_ release the result as an actual package on PyPI (but then why not use https://github.com/briancurtin/deprecation?)

Hi @glizer thanks for jumping right into this. Love the ambition. Before we jump into the code let me collect a couple of my thoughts.

1. I don't like the old striped look. I would like to keep the light/dark theme support that I added. I guess it should be alerting, but not too intrusive. Also, a reason why I used the system font, `sans-serif` will sadly default to the browsers not the OSes default font.
2. Furthermore, I am not really a fan of the close-button. I see that as a potential security risk. However, you added it in a way that only closes it for the current request. That is certainly better than before and OK with me.
3. The dragging seems double trouble. If you want to change the location because it clashes with your design, I would advise users to simply override the template. Since you can hide the notification, this seems even more obsolete. I am also hesitant to introduce JS code, since we would need babel and co for cross browser support.
4. Moving the notification up top is an interesting choice. I would always follow Material, since it's the best tested public interface design framework. In Material, you have banners, however, they don't overlay. That is something that we cannot do, at least not out of the box. Again if you override the template, you can do an integration, that simply moves your content around. Since 90-or-so percent of all websites have a top nav-bar, I would keep the notification on the bottom by default.

Anyhow, those are my humble thoughts. What do you think?
Best,
Joe

I totally buy the boilerplate reduction argument.

The big thing that is missing right now if we go in this direction is our inability to configure things like environment variables in the workspace.yaml file. With the previous method, we had the 'attribute' field that you can set in  your workspace.yaml that tells dagster to scope things down a specific repository every time it loads the code for that repo location in any context. There is no equivalent for env vars, so if you want to start applying this in a production dagster deployment you will need to make sure that the ENVIRONMENT env var is set, both when loading the pipeline code and when launching a run.

In k8s this would look like:

- Configuring the correct user code deployment in the helm chart to set this env var
- Configuring the run launcher in the helm chart to *also* include that env var (and moreover, that would cause it to apply to all runs in that deployment, you will have lost the ability to say that only a specific code location should use that env var).

To me that is a prime motivator for bringing the UserCodeLauncher abstraction that we have in cloud to open source, so that Dagster will have a central place to associate a repository with a given set of config like env vars. After that, you will be able to include env vars in the workspace.yaml, so it would be easy to co-locate an `ENVIRONMENT:prod` config field in the workspace.yaml, just like you can specify an `attribute: prod` in the workspace.yaml today.

I didn't investigate the alternative proposed in https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/1442#issuecomment-1085987284 involving a subclass guaranteeing a `dict`.

While it kinda sucks to add a runtime check to please a linter, this is not on the critical path and users are free not to use it (if they don't use a linter or if they prefer to add a comment on each line where mypy complains to tell it to ignore).

What gives me a small pause is that the [Debian](https://packages.debian.org/buster/python3-parsedatetime)/[Ubuntu](https://packages.ubuntu.com/bionic/python3-parsedatetime) packages for `python3-parsedatetime` install `python-pyicu` (for Python 2) by default (recommends). I'm not sure why. It doesn't make the `icu` module available from Python 3. Was this a packaging mistake, or is there some other side effect here?

Yes, I can confirm that the zip archive linked to from this section doesn't contain the contents of `git` submodules, and this section's instructions therefore don't work.

These instructions were written AT LEAST 5 years ago (https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blame/3eb68e85e99a221b04c0e19eb262e41e210f3da1/docs/Installation-Guide.rst#L59, #945), and maybe even longer back. I suspect that they were written prior to the first `git` submodule being introduced in April 2017 (#368), and have been broken since then. Thanks for catching it!

-----

However, I don't think the proposed fix is the best option.

For people who are already comfortable using `git`, I think the "From Command Line" option should be preferred, and that this "With GUI" option should be reserved for only using pointing and clicking.

I think we should alter the "With GUI" instructions so that the point users to the zip archive attached to each release, which does include all the submodules (as described in https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/issues/5038#issuecomment-1103394333)

That would mean that the "With GUI" instructions are insufficient for building versions of LightGBM other than released versions, but I think that's acceptable in exchange for minimizing the maintenance burden required to keep this documentation up to date. "You have to use `git` if you want to build an unreleased version" seems ok to me.

So instead of this, can you instead please change these instructions to something like the following:

```text
2. Navigate to https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/releases. Choose the release you'd like to build, and download the file with a name like ``LightGBM-complete_source_code_tar_gz.tar.gz``.
```

Would you like to make that change? If you'd prefer not to, I'm happy to open a pull request and `@` you in it for a review.

Got it working; I had to add a `__init__.py` in radis/cython for the first compilation to work on Windows... But the file was previously removed because it created problems on Linux 

Would be nice if someone else can try : @CorentinGrimaldi  ?  


What I managed to do : 

```python

from radis import SpectrumFactory
from radis.test.utils import getTestFile
from radis.tools.database import load_spec
from radis.tools.plot_tools import ParamRange

my_file = getTestFile("CO2_measured_spectrum_4-5um.spec")  # for the example here
s_exp = load_spec(my_file)

s_exp.crop(4120, 4790).plot(Iunit="mW/cm2/sr/nm")


sf = SpectrumFactory(
    2100,
    2400,  # cm-1
    molecule="CO2",
    isotope="1,2,3",
    wstep=0.002,
)

sf.fetch_databank("hitemp")

s = sf.eq_spectrum_gpu_explore(
    var="radiance",
    Tgas=ParamRange(300.0, 2500.0, 1100.0),  # K
    pressure=ParamRange(0.1, 2, 1),  # bar
    mole_fraction=ParamRange(0, 1, 0.8),
    path_length=ParamRange(0, 1, 0.2),  # cm
    slit_FWHM=ParamRange(0, 1.5, 0.24),  # cm-1
    emulate=True,  # runs on CPU
    plotkwargs={"nfig": "same", "wunit": "nm"},
)
```
